summary: Scottish-Italian sculptor, printmaker, and collagist, widely regarded as a pioneer of Pop Art. A founding member of the Independent Group in London. Best known for his collage 'I Was a Rich Man's Plaything' (1947) and his 'As Is When' print series (1965) depicting Ludwig Wittgenstein. His bronze Newton sculpture (1995) stands outside the British Library. Delivered the influential lecture 'Bunk!' at the ICA in 1952, presenting his early collages.

works.name: I Was a Rich Man's Plaything (1947)
works.description: Paolozzi's most famous work, a collage considered one of the first works of Pop Art. It shows a hand holding a gun pointed at a woman posing above a warplane, with the word 'POP' appearing in a cloud of smoke fired from a pistol. The image also contains the text 'Intimate Confessions,' a Coca-Cola advertisement, an advertisement for cherry pie, and the phrase 'Keep 'Em Flying!' above the warplane. Wikipedia

works.name: As Is When (1965)
works.description: A series of silkscreen prints by Paolozzi depicting the philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein in various scenes. One print shows Wittgenstein 'admiring' Betty Hutton and Betty Grable; the accompanying text notes he usually ate pork pies while watching movies in the front row. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Series depicting Ludwig Wittgenstein; one print shows him 'admiring' Betty Hutton and Betty Grable; text says he ate pork pies while watching movies in the front row
examples: This thinker usually ate pork pies while watching movies in the front row, according to the text of Eduardo Paolozzi's painting series As Is When depicting this thinker 'admiring' Betty Hutton and Betty Grable.

works.name: Newton (1995 sculpture)
works.description: A large bronze sculpture outside the British Library in London, inspired by William Blake's monotype of the same name. Blake's print shows Newton naked on a mossy rock, bending down and writing on a scroll with a compass. Paolozzi's sculpture has bolts joining Newton's limbs to his body. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Bronze sculpture outside the British Library; bolts join Newton's limbs to his body; inspired by William Blake's monotype Newton
examples: Bolts join this figure's limbs to his body in an Eduardo Paolozzi sculpture outside the British Library inspired by a print in which this figure sits on an algae-covered rock.
This print was the basis for a bronze sculpture by pop artist Eduardo Paolozzi, found in the piazza outside the British Library.

works.name: Real Gold
works.description: A Pop Art collage by Paolozzi featuring Mickey Mouse alongside a can of lemon juice in front of a woman in a gold swimsuit. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Paolozzi is identified as a pioneer of Pop Art and a founding member of the Independent Group. He delivered the landmark lecture 'Bunk!' at the ICA in 1952, presenting his early collages.

clues.clue: Member of the Independent Group; co-founder of British Pop Art with Richard Hamilton
examples: The origin of this art movement is often traced to a collective of artists including Alison and Peter Smithson and Eduardo Paolozzi who called themselves the Independent Group.
A Lawrence Alloway essay coined the name for this movement, which included former Independent Group members Billy Apple and Eduardo Paolozzi.

clues.clue: Delivered the lecture 'Bunk!' at the ICA, presenting his early Pop Art collages

comprehensive_summary:
Eduardo Paolozzi (1924–2005) was a Scottish-Italian artist celebrated as one of the pioneers of Pop Art. Born in Edinburgh to Italian immigrant parents, he became a central figure in London's post-war avant-garde scene, co-founding the Independent Group at the Institute of Contemporary Arts (ICA) in the early 1950s alongside figures such as Richard Hamilton, Lawrence Alloway, and the Smithsons. His 1952 lecture 'Bunk!' — at which he projected a rapid-fire sequence of American magazine clippings, comic strips, and advertisements — is widely regarded as a foundational moment in Pop Art history.

His most famous work, the collage 'I Was a Rich Man's Plaything' (1947), is considered one of the first Pop Art works. It depicts a woman posing in a red dress above a warplane, a hand holding a gun, the word 'POP' bursting in a cloud of smoke from a pistol, and advertising imagery for Coca-Cola and cherry pie, alongside the text 'Intimate Confessions' and 'Keep 'Em Flying!'. The collage anticipated the movement's characteristic blend of consumer imagery, mass media, and irony.

His 1965 silkscreen print series 'As Is When' depicted the philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein in Pop Art style, with accompanying texts: one print shows Wittgenstein 'admiring' film stars Betty Hutton and Betty Grable, with a text noting he typically ate pork pies while watching movies from the front row of the cinema.

Among his sculptural works, the bronze Newton (1995), installed in the piazza outside the British Library in London, is particularly prominent. Inspired by William Blake's monotype print of the same subject — which shows Newton seated naked on a mossy rock, bending over a scroll with a compass — Paolozzi's version renders Newton with machine-like bolts joining his limbs to his body, reflecting his interest in the relationship between humanity and technology.

His collage 'Real Gold' features Mickey Mouse alongside a can of lemon juice in front of a woman in a gold swimsuit, further demonstrating his recurring use of popular consumer icons. Paolozzi's later career encompassed large-scale public sculptures and continued printmaking, and he was knighted in 1988.
